sentences.text: When I lived on-- in this Home One, somebody suggested that we should talk about our daily lives and maybe put it on paper.
So we got encouraged by our leader, who was Professor Eisinger and a-- and a fellow by the name of Peter Ginz.
He became the editor.
He was one of the older fellows in our home.
And so we started to write this magazine.
And this fellow was a very gifted, very gifted guy.
He would write poems.
He would be painting.
He did all kinds of things.
Peter Ginz, yes.
And-- and the guys in the home contributed to whatever they-- they wanted to.
Some of the guys, we had some guys who were very good poets.
There was a guy by the name of Hanus Hachenburg, a young guy was-- who was-- wrote a lot of poetry.
Another guy was [INAUDIBLE] Ernest.
His name was Orenstein.
He-- he-- he changed his name.
He wrote also a lot of poetry.
And various guys as they were-- were going through their life in-- in the ghetto, they wrote about their experiences throughout the camp.
And I contributed some-- some of the articles, too, some of the paintings.
And the book was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at the end of the of the war what happened, I-- there was nobody-- nobody in the camp but me.
So I I was the only survivor.
And in order-- if somebody would have found out that we were writing something like this, we all would have been sent into a transport and-- and-- right away.
And the magazine would have been confiscated.
So at the end of the war when nobody was already contributing-- I was the only survivor.
I picked up this magazine and buried it by my father's, by the blacksmith's, right on top of behind the blacksmith's on the walls.
And-- and after the war, I brought it to Prague.
And-- Did the magazine have a name?
The magazine-- yes.
The magazine, the name was [NON-ENGLISH].
It was really-- the abbreviation was [NON-ENGLISH]. which was a school according to Dostoevsky.
Dostoevsky-- at one time, there was a school in Russia for-- for-- for kids who didn't have any parents.
And-- and this was molded after that, because we were like orphans.
So we called this magazine [NON-ENGLISH].
And it was called we lead.
We are in the lead.
And--
On the magazine was yes.
The home which we had was this [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], Dostoevsky which was-- which was publishing this-- we were publishing this every week.
The guys would contribute.
And Fridays, we would sit on the bunks.
And the guys who contributed these articles, they would read them.
And we would switch writing some of it.
At the beginning, we had some typewriter.
Somebody brought us somewhere, found us a typewriter.
And we were typing it, but we ran out of ribbons.
We didn't have any more ribbons, so we started to-- to to write it, hand.
They are-- most of the pages are handwritten or people were doing the pictures by hand and this is how.
There is only-- only one copy.
There is only one document.
I don't know if any copies were made.
I-- at the end of the war, I dug it out.
As a matter of fact, it was buried with the ashes of my grandmother who died over there.
And at the end of the war when the Germans-- there must have been-- the urns were stored in one of these catacombs.
And at the end of the war, the Germans didn't want anybody to find out how many people died in there.
So we were evacuating all these-- all these urns from these.
And I was assigned with the horses with a wagon to-- people were loading these urns onto the wagons.
I would take the urns outside the camp.
And an SS man would then take by horses and bring it to the Oder River.
And then they would dump it into the Oder River.
And because I was acquainted with a guy as I was bringing bodies to the crematorium, I was acquainted with a guy.
So my father said when his mother died, when my grandmother died, that he's going to give him a wooden-- he will make up a wooden box instead of these paper.
These boxes were like, maybe 10 inches by-- not even that, 10 centimeters by 10 centimeters by 10 centimeters white boxes with a name on it and-- and numbered.
Of the--
Well, it was every week.
I don't recall how many issues.
But it continued for quite a while, maybe two years, something like that.
And we were doing it for every-- every week.
The issue was-- sometimes it was 10 pages.
Sometimes it was less sometimes it was more.
It depended--
--how many people contributed.
And all the original, there was only one one copy made of each.
Only-- only one copy.
No, no, what happened is on Fridays we would get together and the people would be sitting around on the bunks.
And the people who contributed the articles, they would read them.
It was not seen.
Very few-- very few people knew about it.
Mostly, the people who lived in the building, the L417, they knew that he had a magazine.
I am not sure that some of the other-- for example, there was a-- home number five was a home which was a Zionist home.
It was called [NON-ENGLISH].
They called themselves [NON-ENGLISH].
I don't know what it is, a lion I think it is, right?
I know it's Hebrew, but I'm not--
It's Hebrew, but I am not so sure what it--
I'm not sure it's lion.
I-- I'm not sure-- sure what it means.
And they-- those guys really, we were competing with them.
Because we are non-Zionist.
And these guys were all mostly Zionist.
And we were competing with them, so when we, for example, play soccer.
I am not sure whether they didn't have anything like that.
But, unfortunately, nobody survived from that home.
And-- and I don't think anything survived, because--
I was able--
I was able to save the entire-- because Peter Ginz was holding always on it.
And Peter used to live-- we had bunks with-- where six people would live.
And I used to live under him.
And when he went into the camp, the magazine was still surviving, but really just dying.
Because he was really the moving force behind it.
And then after that, I just picked-- nobody was around.
So I picked the magazine.
And I said, well, maybe if we survive, we will have something out of it.
So at the end of the war, I picked it up and I brought it-- brought it to Prague.
And some of the-- there was an orphanage, a Jewish orphanage.
And when I was going, I didn't know really what to do with that magazine.
And I was going to United States.
So I was saying, well, I can't take it to United States, because it's written in Czech.
And now it has to be translated if anything.
Maybe it can serve better here in Czechoslovakia.
So I brought it to-- to-- to this Jewish orphanage in Prague.
And some of my friends lived there, some of the kids who who returned from different camps, kids I I used to know.
And there was a lady who used to have her son on our-- on our-- in our home.
Her name was Mrs.
Lowe ?].
And I gave it to-- to her.
I said, look, you know, here is a magazine, which we were issuing.
She was-- she was aware of it.
Because she used to come in and sometimes clean out in her house.
She was helping out with cleaning.
And so I gave it to her.
But she never remembered who gave it to her.
Well, one day there was a lady who took interest in this magazine.
And I got a letter that they would like to find out.
It's the only things that could have happened is how the magazine survived.
I would-- she would like to find out.
And I wrote her how the magazine survived.
And I guess in the magazine, when they-- when they issued some of the pages of the magazine in Czech, she put the letter which I wrote to her.

And-- and also, lately it was translated into English through big effort of this fellow, George Brady, who really deserves credit for bringing it-- having it translated into English and bringing it here.
And-- and that's how the magazine is.
The magazine, I I don't know exactly how it got-- got there.
But the magazine right now is stored in the-- in a museum in Theresienstadt.
From the home, as I understand it, where we-- where we used to live, they made a museum.
Before, when the communists were in power, that home was some kind of a military museum.
And I am not sure where the magazine was during the war.
Because the communists wouldn't allow it to be published.
During the communist-- I don't mean during the war.
During the communist rule in Czechoslovakia, they didn't allow it to be published.
There was an effort by this fellow [INAUDIBLE] who was also one of-- of the fellows who-- who were in this Home One, to try to get it published.
But it never materialized until later it was-- it was published.
And it's published now in German, I think, and in English and in Czech.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
Right.
Well, most of the--
Most of the fellows, all of the fellows were transported to Auschwitz.
And from [NON-ENGLISH].
From Auschwitz, they went into different camps.
They spread out to Bergen-Belsen, some of them, some of them, you know, all over the place.
And then they survived.
They survived in these camps, and they came back to Prague again after the war.
And there are not too many left.
I guess there were about 12 or something like that who survived this all.
And at one time through the home, we must have had more than 100 people go through the home.
Correct.
Correct.
Yes, some contributed.
There was a fellow by the name of Orenstein, Ernest, Zdenek Ernest, who was one of the contributors.
There was another fellow, Kudoch, Kud Kudoch, who lives in Prague.
I think one of the fellow who might have contributed is George Brady, too.
But George Brady was involved mostly in manual work.
And he wasn't the type of writing.
Yes.
Right.
Correct.
Correct.
That's the only copy.
I haven't-- I haven't seen it myself since the time I-- I brought it to-- to that orphanage.
And maybe next year, I will try to go to Theresienstadt and see it.

Well, I guess, I said [INAUDIBLE] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at-- at the beginning, I was very, very upset when I seen people being carried, dead people being carried on these little wagons into the morgues.
And-- and then on top of it, bringing even bodies with 50 dead people on a flatbed to the crematorium or even to be buried.
After a while, you, I guess, the human being becomes used to anything.
And I just became just, like, oblivious to anything that was happening around.
Yes.
There is a story behind it.
I was-- I was put into a-- into a transport.
And what happened is my father told me, look, I will try to talk to the SS man, Rahm, and tell him that my son is in the-- in the transport and if he could take you out.
If he-- and what-- what happened is there were some reviews of people.
And we went in front of Rahm.
And I came in front of him.
And I said, I am the son of the blacksmith, Taussig.
And I lied to him, I work in the blacksmith's also.
And-- and he-- because my father was a real good blacksmith and he was hoofing his horses, and out of mercy, I think, he took me out.
So my father was the really one who saved me from going to-- to the camp.
Otherwise, I would have been in the camp, too.
Rahm was the camp commandant.
And-- and he once in a while, you know, brought sometimes the horse to the-- to the blacksmith's also.
And he-- he sort of knew my father, that he was able to to fix the horses right away.
And so he-- he-- he saved me.
OK.
One day-- one day, all of a sudden, we had a shooting around-- around the camp.
And all of a sudden, news spread that the Russians are in the area.
And I used to have another guy who would be helping me out cleaning the horses in the morning.
And all of a sudden, he said-- this guy was a real adventurist.
He lost his parents.
He didn't have anybody.
He was-- he was the only one.
He was an orphan.
And he said, I'm going to go out, out of the camp.
And he went out of the camp.
And he found that there were Russian tanks around-- around the ghetto.
And somehow the Russians adopted him.
And everyday, all of a sudden he disappeared.
He went with the Russians.
In the evening, he-- he came back.
And-- and they were giving him food.
And so-- and then things spread out.
You know, we are free.
The only thing is there was, at that time, there was some-- there was typhoid.
And people-- people didn't-- they didn't want people to-- to get out of the ghetto right away.
But I had a pair of horses.
And I-- my father said, well, we are not going to wait for anybody.
We just load up-- load up whatever we-- we have, mother, my-- my sister.
And I had two friends, one of them, [INAUDIBLE], he is in Boston here.
Another one is in Israel, this fellow, [INAUDIBLE], we went.
They wanted to go.
So we-- we got on that wagon and just drove the horses at night towards Prague.
And-- the Germans, the Germans, what happened is the Germans packed up some of the horses.
We had-- I think we had maybe 10 pair of horses.
The-- the horses which I had were older pair of horses.
Because as I said, my uncle was in charge.
And some of the horses were really thrifty.
And he didn't want me to get hurt with them.
So he gave me older horses.
These horses were maybe like 12 years-- 12 years old.
So they were not as thrifty.
So the Germans didn't take those horses.
The majority of the horses, they take.
And they packed up before the Russians came in, loaded up whatever they had and just-- just cleaned out of the-- or they disappeared from there.
So there was really nobody in charge of-- of the-- of the people in the ghetto.
They-- they completely disappeared.
And we just packed up and went-- went to Prague.
On the way--
Me, my father, my sister and two friends, we went to Prague.
And we traveled at night.
We bumped into Russian soldiers.
They gave us marmalade.
Somewhere they stole-- stole somewhere barrels of marmalade.
Gave us marmalade, they gave us some food.
And we traveled throughout the whole night from Theresienstadt to Prague.

And as I mentioned previously, as we were approaching our home, it's like an-- a street going up, a long street going up towards our house, the people started to recognize my-- my father, some of these.
And this word spread around that the Taussig family is coming-- coming home.
And this fellow I mentioned before, who was the guy who chased us from the-- from the street, somehow found-- word got to him, and he hung himself in the house.
And and the-- then the widow two days later came to my father and asked him if he would be so kind and forgave him and take him to the cemetery.
So my father, you know, acquiesced to it and-- and took his body to cemetery.
There was nobody in our home.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we were able to just-- first, we had a little-- little-- a little room.
Apparently, some Germans must have been living there.
And they also cleared out.
And there was nobody.
There was nothing in the house.
It was just the empty house.
All the furniture was gone.
So what happened is we slowly-- my father slowly started to get things together.
And finally we-- and we found out that my uncle who had his business, about a mile-- several miles away from us didn't return.
Nobody from his brothers and sisters came back.
Everybody was dead.
So we moved later on into my uncle's house, because it was a larger, larger home.
The one of my uncle who was in charge of the agriculture, he returned also home.
But he didn't live in Prague.
He lived in Turnov, which is on the northern side of Bohemia.
And how long did you remain in Prague?
And when did you determine to leave Prague?
When I came-- when I came to Prague, this was in May.
And since I was able to go to school and-- and, you know, attend some classes in in the ghetto, I was taught by people, I-- I went back to my gymnasium.
And this was the-- at that time it was [NON-ENGLISH], which is the sixth grade.
And since I was up to date and this was just about at the end of the-- of the-- I was even ahead of in mathematics, for example, and physics.
Because these guys, I-- I told you, some of these teachers, they really taught us.
I took the test in mathematics with the-- with the kids.
And I had A out of it.
And the teacher, my teacher, she burst into-- started to cry.
And she said, here is a-- here is a guy who, you know, was in a camp.
And and some of the guys who were over here.
And he made A and some of you didn't.
And she got a liking to me.
And and I continued in the gymnasium.
I finished, graduated from the gymnasium.
And- and then at the end, my uncle, who was here in New York, brought-- gave me a visa to my sister and myself.
And we came to New York.
And my parents had an arrangement with the, at that time, ambassador in Canada.
He gave them a visa to Canada.
And they came to Canada.
And later on, I brought my parents over here to New York.
Well, when I came here-- Yeah.
When I came here, naturally, I-- I didn't-- I had to either go to school or go to work.
Well, I decided I'll go to work and go to school at night.
And I started to-- first, I-- I lived with my uncle, who lived on Park Avenue in New York.
And he had a business importing watches and building watches.
He's somewhere in Connecticut.
And I-- I went into the shipping department.
I was helping with packaging and stuff like that and delivering packages in New York.
And then I said, you know, that's not a job which I want to do all my life.
So I went also on my own.
I used to live with him on Park Avenue, and then I moved into Yorkville section in-- in Manhattan.
I got acquainted with some of the Czech fellas.
I-- started-- I was a good soccer player, so I started to play soccer.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I started to polish furniture to make some money.
And then the Korean War broke out, and I-- I was eligible to go into Army.
I said, well, maybe I go into the Air Force first, volunteer.
I volunteer, I went into the Air Force.
I stayed in the Air Force.
I went through-- through Air Force radar-- radar school.
And I was assigned to [INAUDIBLE] installing radars.
[INAUDIBLE] On St.
Albans in Vermont, one-- one section.
And then I was discharged.
And I-- then I got a job working as a technician and making fuel gauges for a company called [INAUDIBLE] I stayed with.
And in the meantime, I went to school at night to Brooklyn Polytech.
And-- and I became an engineer in this company.
After 13 years, the company went to bankruptcy.
I worked for Kollsman.
I worked most of my life in defense, working anywhere from radars to radar warning systems and sonars.
And then towards the end, I was in management for a company called General Instrument.
And at the end, we were sold out to Litton, and I went into retirement.
As I was playing soccer, I got acquainted on the field with my wife.
And we got-- we got married.
I was 23, and she was 18.
And we got married.
First, we had a small apartment.
And we both saved up some money.
We bought a six-family house, because I was saying on engineering salary.
I don't know if I can afford a one-family house.
So we bought a six-family house.
And later on, we-- we kept the six-family house.
We bought a house in Plainview.
And I sold the six-family house.
And then we lived in Plainview, I think, for 13 years.
That's where most our kids went to-- our three kids went to schools, went through the schools.
And after they went through the schools, we moved for a while to Massachusetts.
I was a program manager on a radaring job at Raytheon in Massachusetts.
And we didn't like it too much in Massachusetts, so we moved back to Long Island and moved to Huntington.
And that's where we are right now.
And I went to work for General Instrument.
That's where I wound up staying all my life.
And my kids, my oldest daughter, she went to-- to Plainview High School.
And she went through Stony Brook University, and then she went to Germany.
She became a language teacher.
She got a master's degree at Stony Brook.
My son went to Columbia.
And then he got a scholarship to Stanford University.
He got his PhD at Stanford.
And now he has a lab at University of Michigan and teaches also in Michigan.
My youngest daughter, she went for-- she was in gymnastics, became a Phys Ed teacher.
And also, her second degree is in software.
But she wound up making software, and she married.
And now she is a housewife.
She just stays at home.
OK.
